title: Peacock pattern marbling
subtitle: Producing the curved fan-like peacock design on a carrageenan size
excerpt: The peacock pattern is one of the most recognisable designs in the Western marbling tradition. It begins as a simple nonpareil (fine-combed stripe) and is then converted into arching fan shapes by drawing a wide-toothed rake through the stripes in a series of curves. This tutorial covers the sequence from colour laying to raking to lifting the sheet.
sourceNotes: Anne Chambers, A Practical Guide to Marbling Paper (1986, Thames and Hudson), chapter on combed and raked patterns. The peacock rake sequence follows Chambers's instructions for the Antique Spot pattern converted to Peacock.

glossaryTerms.definition: A fine-combed marbling pattern consisting of alternating thin stripes produced by two passes of the comb at 90° to each other. The base pattern for many more complex designs, including peacock.
The viscous water bath made from carrageenan seaweed extract that holds marbling pigments on the surface. The size must be made 24 hours in advance to reach full viscosity.

content.text: The peacock pattern builds in stages: first the

base, then the wide-toothed rake pass that converts the stripes into arching fans. The key variable is the spacing of the wide rake: teeth too close together produce a dense pattern with no breathing room; teeth too far apart lose the interlocking fan effect.

24 hours before use: dissolve 5 g of carrageenan powder per litre of warm water using an immersion blender. Pour into the tray and leave overnight at room temperature; the viscosity rises as it cools. The size is ready when a small amount poured from a height falls in a slow, slightly syrupy stream. Skim any foam from the surface before marbling.

Brush the face side of each sheet with a dilute alum solution (15 g potash alum per litre of water) using a wide foam brush. Work quickly to coat the whole sheet. Dry the sheets flat, face up, for at least 15 minutes. Marbled pigment will only adhere to the alum-mordanted side; if you marble on the wrong side the pattern washes off when you rinse the sheet.

Use an eyedropper to add one drop of ox-gall to each colour cup and stir. Test each colour by dropping one drop from 10 cm above the size: it should spread to a circle of about 5 cm. If it sinks, add more ox-gall; if it spreads to the tray walls, the size is too thin, remix. Drop colours alternately across the tray in parallel strips, working quickly: four or five colours in repeated order.

Drag the fine-toothed comb across the tray from left to right (perpendicular to the colour strips). Then drag the comb from right to left in the other direction, 5 mm offset. The result is the nonpareil grid of fine interlocking stripes.

Make or use a wide-toothed rake with teeth spaced 30-40 mm apart. Draw the rake across the combed pattern in a series of shallow S-curves from left to right: enter at the top, sweep down-right, then back up-right, repeating across the full length of the tray. Work in one continuous slow movement. The nonpareil stripes pull into the characteristic arching fan shapes. Immediately lay the prepared paper on the surface, alum-side down, and press from the centre out with the tips of your fingers.

Lift the sheet from one end and rinse gently under cool running water from a low tap. Hold the sheet at one edge and let water run across the surface to clear residual size. Clip to dry, pattern face outward. Skim the tray with a sheet of newspaper before the next pull.
